news_summary: New NASA studies reveal the potential for ancient life to endure long-term in Martian ice and explore the effects of space on fermented foods.
news_summary_fr: De nouvelles études de la NASA révèlent le potentiel de survie à long terme de formes de vie anciennes dans la glace martienne et explorent les effets de l'espace sur les aliments fermentés.
news_summary_es: Nuevos estudios de la NASA revelan el potencial de la vida antigua para perdurar a largo plazo en el hielo marciano y exploran los efectos del espacio en los alimentos fermentados.

articles.title: NASA study finds ancient life could survive 50 million years in Martian ice
articles.summary: Mars’ frozen ice caps may be time capsules for ancient life. Lab experiments show that key building blocks of proteins can survive tens of millions of years in pure ice, even under relentless cosmic radiation. Ice mixed with Martian-like soil, however, destroys organic material far more quickly. The findings point future missions toward drilling into clean, buried ice rather than studying rocks or dirt.
articles.content:
Future missions to Mars may want to dig into ice rather than rock. Scientists say ancient microbes, or traces of them, could be locked inside Martian ice deposits, preserved for tens of millions of years.

Researchers from NASA Goddard Space Flight Center and Penn State recreated Mars like conditions in the laboratory to test that idea. They found that pieces of amino acids from E. coli bacteria, if trapped in Martian permafrost or ice caps, could survive more than 50 million years even under constant cosmic radiation. The findings, published in Astrobiology, suggest that missions searching for life on Mars should prioritize pure ice or ice rich permafrost instead of focusing mainly on rocks, clay, or soil.

"Fifty million years is far greater than the expected age for some current surface ice deposits on Mars, which are often less than two million years old, meaning any organic life present within the ice would be preserved," said co author Christopher House, professor of geosciences, affiliate of the Huck Institutes of the Life Sciences and the Earth and Environment Systems Institute, and director of the Penn State Consortium for Planetary and Exoplanetary Science and Technology. "That means if there are bacteria near the surface of Mars, future missions can find it."

Simulating Mars and Cosmic Radiation in the Lab

The study was led by Alexander Pavlov, a space scientist at NASA Goddard who completed a doctorate in geosciences at Penn State in 2001. The team sealed E. coli bacteria inside test tubes filled with pure water ice. Other samples were combined with water and materials commonly found in Martian sediment, including silicate based rocks and clay.

The frozen samples were placed in a gamma radiation chamber at Penn State's Radiation Science and Engineering Center. The chamber was cooled to minus 60 degrees Fahrenheit to match temperatures in icy regions of Mars. The bacteria were then exposed to radiation equivalent to 20 million years of cosmic ray bombardment on the Martian surface. Afterward, the samples were vacuum sealed and shipped back to NASA Goddard under cold conditions for amino acid testing. Researchers then modeled an additional 30 years of radiation exposure, bringing the total to 50 million years.

Pure Ice Protects Organic Molecules

The results were striking. In pure water ice, more than 10 percent of the amino acids, which are the building blocks of proteins, survived the full 50 million year simulation. By contrast, samples mixed with Mars like sediment broke down 10 times faster and did not survive.

A 2022 study by the same team had shown that amino acids preserved in a mixture of 10% water ice and 90% Martian soil were destroyed more quickly than samples containing only sediment.

"Based on the 2022 study findings, it was thought that organic material in ice or water alone would be destroyed even more rapidly than the 10% water mixture," Pavlov said. "So, it was surprising to find that the organic materials placed in water ice alone are destroyed at a much slower rate than the samples containing water and soil."

Researchers think the faster breakdown in mixed samples may happen because a thin film forms where ice touches minerals. That layer could allow radiation to move more freely and damage amino acids.

"While in solid ice, harmful particles created by radiation get frozen in place and may not be able to reach organic compounds," Pavlov said. "These results suggest that pure ice or ice-dominated regions are an ideal place to look for recent biological material on Mars."

Implications for Europa and Enceladus

The team also tested organic material at temperatures similar to those on Europa, an icy moon of Jupiter, and Enceladus, an icy moon of Saturn. At those even colder temperatures, deterioration slowed down further.

Pavlov said the findings are encouraging for NASA's Europa Clipper mission, which will study Europa's ice shell and subsurface ocean. Europa is the fourth largest of Jupiter's 95 moons. Europa Clipper launched in 2024 and is traveling 1.8 billion miles to reach Jupiter in 2030. The spacecraft will perform 49 close flybys to determine whether environments beneath the surface could support life.

Drilling Into Martian Ice

When it comes to Mars, accessing buried ice will require the right tools. The 2008 NASA Mars Phoenix mission was the first to dig down and photograph ice in the Martian equivalent of the Arctic Circle.

"There is a lot of ice on Mars, but most of it is just below the surface," House said. "Future missions need a large enough drill or a powerful scoop to access it, similar to the design and capabilities of Phoenix."

In addition to House and Pavlov, the research team included Zhidan Zhang, a retired lab technologist in the Penn State Department of Geosciences, along with Hannah McLain, Kendra Farnsworth, Daniel Glavin, Jamie Elsila, and Jason Dworkin of NASA Goddard.

The work was funded by NASA's Planetary Science Division Internal Scientist Funding Program through the Fundamental Laboratory Research work package at Goddard Space Flight Center.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery with broad implications for the search for ancient life on Mars and other icy worlds, highlighting a promising new direction for future space missions. The study provides detailed experimental evidence that organic molecules can survive for tens of millions of years in pure Martian ice, which could guide exploration strategies and increase the chances of finding preserved life signs. This breakthrough has meaningful impact on astrobiology, planetary science, and humanity's understanding of life beyond Earth.
articles.personality_title: NASA study shows ancient life could survive millions of years in Martian ice
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists want to find signs of ancient life on Mars. Mars has ice caps and frozen soil that might hold clues about past life. Knowing where to look can help future space missions find these clues.

**What happened** – Researchers from NASA and Penn State tested how long pieces of bacteria could last in ice like that on Mars. They froze bacteria in pure water ice and in ice mixed with soil similar to Mars. Then, they exposed the samples to radiation like what is found on Mars for up to 50 million years. They found that in pure ice, important parts of the bacteria survived much longer than in ice mixed with soil.

**Impact** – This discovery shows that pure ice on Mars protects organic molecules better than soil or rocks. It means future missions should focus on drilling into clean ice to find signs of ancient life. This is different from past missions that mainly looked at rocks and dirt. It also suggests that icy moons like Europa and Enceladus might protect life in similar ways.

**What's next step** – Scientists want to develop tools to dig into Mars’ ice deeper, like a strong drill similar to one used in a past Mars mission. NASA’s upcoming missions may use these findings to search in the right places. Meanwhile, the study also supports missions like Europa Clipper, which will explore an icy moon of Jupiter for signs of life.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Pure ice on Mars can preserve signs of ancient life for millions of years, guiding future missions to search beneath the ice rather than in soil or rocks.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une étude de la NASA montre que la vie ancienne pourrait survivre des millions d'années dans la glace martienne
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques cherchent des traces de vie ancienne sur Mars. La planète a des calottes glaciaires et du sol gelé qui pourraient contenir des indices sur une vie passée. Savoir où chercher peut aider les futures missions spatiales.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Des chercheurs de la NASA et de Penn State ont testé combien de temps des morceaux de bactéries pouvaient durer dans de la glace semblable à celle de Mars. Ils ont congelé des bactéries dans de la glace pure et dans de la glace mélangée à un sol similaire à Mars. Puis, ils ont exposé les échantillons à des radiations comme celles sur Mars pendant jusqu’à 50 millions d’années. Ils ont découvert que dans la glace pure, des parties importantes des bactéries survivaient beaucoup plus longtemps que dans la glace mélangée au sol.

**Impact** – Cette découverte montre que la glace pure sur Mars protège mieux les molécules organiques que le sol ou les roches. Cela signifie que les futures missions devraient se concentrer sur le forage dans la glace propre pour trouver des signes de vie ancienne. C’est différent des missions passées qui regardaient surtout les roches et la terre. Cela suggère aussi que des lunes glacées comme Europe et Encelade pourraient protéger la vie de façon similaire.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques veulent développer des outils pour creuser plus profondément dans la glace de Mars, comme un foret puissant utilisé lors d’une mission martienne précédente. Les missions NASA à venir pourraient utiliser ces résultats pour chercher aux bons endroits. En même temps, cette étude soutient aussi des missions comme Europa Clipper, qui explorera une lune glacée de Jupiter à la recherche de vie.

**Résumé en une phrase** – La glace pure sur Mars peut préserver des signes de vie ancienne pendant des millions d’années, guidant les futures missions à chercher sous la glace plutôt que dans le sol ou les roches.
articles.personality_title_es: Estudio de la NASA muestra que la vida antigua podría sobrevivir millones de años en el hielo marciano
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos quieren encontrar señales de vida antigua en Marte. Marte tiene casquetes de hielo y suelo congelado que podrían contener pistas sobre vida pasada. Saber dónde buscar puede ayudar a futuras misiones espaciales.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de la NASA y Penn State probaron cuánto tiempo pueden durar partes de bacterias en hielo parecido al de Marte. Congelaron bacterias en hielo puro y en hielo mezclado con suelo similar al marciano. Luego, expusieron las muestras a radiación como la que hay en Marte durante hasta 50 millones de años. Descubrieron que en el hielo puro, partes importantes de las bacterias sobrevivieron mucho más tiempo que en el hielo mezclado con suelo.

**Impacto** – Este hallazgo muestra que el hielo puro en Marte protege mejor las moléculas orgánicas que el suelo o las rocas. Esto significa que las futuras misiones deberían enfocarse en perforar hielo limpio para buscar señales de vida antigua. Esto es diferente a misiones anteriores que se enfocaban principalmente en rocas y tierra. También sugiere que lunas heladas como Europa y Encélado podrían proteger la vida de manera similar.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos quieren crear herramientas para excavar más profundo en el hielo de Marte, como un taladro fuerte usado en una misión pasada. Las próximas misiones de la NASA podrían usar estos resultados para buscar en los lugares correctos. Al mismo tiempo, este estudio también apoya misiones como Europa Clipper, que explorará una luna helada de Júpiter en busca de vida.

**Resumen en una frase** – El hielo puro en Marte puede conservar señales de vida antigua durante millones de años, guiando futuras misiones a buscar bajo el hielo en lugar de en el suelo o las rocas.
articles.image_prompt: A serene Martian icy landscape bathed in soft warm light, featuring translucent blue ice blocks embedded with faint glowing molecular structures symbolizing ancient microbes, with a distant silhouette of a futuristic drill gently breaking the ice surface under a calm reddish sky.

articles.title: NASA Study to Analyze Fermented Food Samples from Space
articles.summary: Certain nutrients critical for human health lack the shelf life needed to span multi-year missions to the Moon, Mars, and beyond. NASA’s BioNutrients-3 is part of an experiment series testing ways to use microorganisms to produce these nutrients in space and on demand. The on-demand nature of this experiment is similar to making nutrient-dense fermented […]
articles.content:
Astronaut and Expedition 73 Flight Engineer Kimiya Yui of JAXA (Japan Aerospace Exploration Agency) displays production bags containing probiotic yogurt cultures for the BioNutrients-3 investigation on Oct. 2, 2025, aboard the International Space Station. NASA

Certain nutrients critical for human health lack the shelf life needed to span multi-year missions to the Moon, Mars, and beyond. NASA’s BioNutrients-3 is part of an experiment series testing ways to use microorganisms to produce these nutrients in space and on demand.

The on-demand nature of this experiment is similar to making nutrient-dense fermented foods on Earth, such as how milk is transformed by good bacteria into yogurt. But in this case, there is a focus on producing specific types and quantities of nutrients essential for future space explorers.

Samples from BioNutrients-3, along with other valuable experiments, are set to return from the International Space Station aboard a SpaceX Dragon spacecraft supporting the company’s 33rd commercial resupply mission for NASA. The spacecraft is set to depart the space station on Thursday, Feb. 26 for its return to Earth. Watch NASA’s live coverage of the undocking and departure starting at 11:45 a.m. EST on NASA+, Amazon Prime, and the agency’s YouTube channel.

Once the samples return to Earth, the science team at NASA’s Ames Research Center in California’s Silicon Valley will perform analysis procedures. Results from this study can help NASA develop methods to produce vital nutrients that could support human deep space exploration as part of NASA’s Artemis campaign.

NASA’s BioNutrients-3 is part of the Synthetic Biology project, which is funded by the Game Changing Development program within NASA’s Space Technology Mission Directorate.
articles.reasoning: The article describes a NASA experiment with significant potential impact on human health and sustainability for long-duration space missions, addressing a critical challenge for deep space exploration. It focuses on a clear scientific advancement with broad implications for future space travelers, providing sufficient detail about the experiment and its purpose.
articles.personality_title: NASA studies making fresh nutrients in space with fermented foods
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Long space missions to the Moon, Mars, and beyond need fresh and healthy food. But some important nutrients spoil before the journey ends. NASA wants to find a way to make these nutrients during the trip.

**What happened** – NASA’s BioNutrients-3 experiment tested using tiny living things called microorganisms to create important nutrients on the International Space Station (ISS). Astronaut Kimiya Yui showed how probiotic yogurt cultures were made in space on October 2, 2025. The experiment works like how milk turns into yogurt on Earth but focuses on making specific nutrients astronauts need.

**Impact** – This experiment could solve a big problem: how to keep astronauts healthy on missions lasting years. Making nutrients on demand means food can stay fresh and nutritious without needing to carry everything from Earth. This is a step toward safer, longer space travel.

**What's next step** – The experiment samples will return to Earth on February 26, 2026, aboard a SpaceX Dragon spacecraft. Scientists at NASA’s Ames Research Center will study them to learn how well the process worked. The findings will help NASA plan better food systems for future space missions, including the Artemis program to the Moon.

**One-sentence takeaway** – NASA’s experiment with fermented foods in space shows a new way to produce fresh nutrients for astronauts on long missions.
articles.personality_title_fr: La NASA étudie la fabrication de nutriments frais dans l’espace grâce aux aliments fermentés
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les longues missions spatiales vers la Lune, Mars et au-delà nécessitent des aliments frais et sains. Mais certains nutriments importants se détériorent avant la fin du voyage. La NASA cherche un moyen de fabriquer ces nutriments pendant le trajet.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – L’expérience BioNutrients-3 de la NASA a testé l’utilisation de micro-organismes pour créer des nutriments essentiels à bord de la Station spatiale internationale (ISS). L’astronaute Kimiya Yui a montré comment des cultures de yaourt probiotiques ont été produites dans l’espace le 2 octobre 2025. L’expérience fonctionne comme la transformation du lait en yaourt sur Terre, mais se concentre sur la fabrication de nutriments spécifiques nécessaires aux astronautes.

**Impact** – Cette expérience pourrait résoudre un grand problème : comment garder les astronautes en bonne santé lors de missions de plusieurs années. Produire des nutriments à la demande signifie que la nourriture peut rester fraîche et nutritive sans tout transporter depuis la Terre. C’est une avancée pour des voyages spatiaux plus sûrs et plus longs.

**Prochaine étape** – Les échantillons de l’expérience reviendront sur Terre le 26 février 2026 à bord d’un vaisseau SpaceX Dragon. Les scientifiques du Centre de recherche Ames de la NASA les étudieront pour voir l’efficacité du procédé. Les résultats aideront la NASA à mieux préparer les systèmes alimentaires pour les futures missions spatiales, y compris le programme Artemis vers la Lune.

**Résumé en une phrase** – L’expérience de la NASA sur les aliments fermentés dans l’espace montre une nouvelle façon de produire des nutriments frais pour les astronautes lors de longues missions.
articles.personality_title_es: La NASA estudia cómo producir nutrientes frescos en el espacio con alimentos fermentados
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Las misiones espaciales largas hacia la Luna, Marte y más allá necesitan alimentos frescos y saludables. Pero algunos nutrientes importantes se echan a perder antes de que termine el viaje. La NASA quiere encontrar una forma de hacer estos nutrientes durante la misión.

**Qué pasó** – El experimento BioNutrients-3 de la NASA probó usar microorganismos para crear nutrientes importantes en la Estación Espacial Internacional (ISS). El astronauta Kimiya Yui mostró cómo se produjeron cultivos de yogur probiótico en el espacio el 2 de octubre de 2025. El experimento funciona como cuando la leche se convierte en yogur en la Tierra, pero se enfoca en producir nutrientes específicos que los astronautas necesitan.

**Impacto** – Este experimento podría resolver un gran problema: cómo mantener saludables a los astronautas en misiones que duran años. Producir nutrientes bajo demanda significa que la comida puede mantenerse fresca y nutritiva sin tener que llevar todo desde la Tierra. Esto es un avance para viajes espaciales más seguros y largos.

**Próximo paso** – Las muestras del experimento regresarán a la Tierra el 26 de febrero de 2026 en una nave espacial SpaceX Dragon. Los científicos del Centro de Investigación Ames de la NASA las analizarán para aprender qué tan bien funcionó el proceso. Los resultados ayudarán a la NASA a planificar mejores sistemas de alimentos para futuras misiones espaciales, incluido el programa Artemisa a la Luna.

**Frase clave** – El experimento de la NASA con alimentos fermentados en el espacio muestra una nueva forma de producir nutrientes frescos para astronautas en misiones largas.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a glowing probiotic yogurt culture bag floating gently inside a softly illuminated space station module, with stylized symbolic representations of beneficial bacteria swirling inside the bag, set against a calm backdrop of Earth visible through a window, rendered in natural, muted tones.
